meta.name: Roti (Chapati)
meta.subtitle: Soft whole-wheat flatbread cooked on a hot tawa/skillet

source.name: SSA canonical cuisines/indian/recipes/roti.json
notes: Deterministic roti method with clear doneness checks for softness and puff.
Designed for standard US kitchens (cast iron or nonstick works).

summary: Classic Indian roti (chapati): whole-wheat dough rested briefly, rolled thin, and cooked on a hot skillet until spotted and softly puffed.

optional.label: Tawa (flat griddle)
optional.notes: A tawa is traditional; a cast-iron skillet works great.

optional.label: Tongs
optional.notes: Useful if finishing directly over flame for puffing.

notes: Contains wheat (gluten).

ingredients.label: Whole wheat flour (atta preferred)
notes: If using standard whole wheat flour, sift once for softer roti.
Atta is milled finer and yields softer rotis.

ingredients.label: Neutral oil (optional)
notes: Optional; a small amount can soften dough.
You may also brush finished rotis with ghee or butter (not vegan).

ingredients.label: Warm water
notes: Add gradually; humidity and flour type change the exact amount.
Target: soft, pliable dough—not sticky.

steps.title: Mix the dough
instructions: In a bowl, combine whole wheat flour and salt (if using).
Add warm water gradually while mixing until a shaggy dough forms.
checks: Dough should come together without dry flour pockets.
If sticky, dust with a little flour; if dry, add water 1 tsp at a time.

steps.title: Knead until smooth
instructions: Knead 6–8 minutes until the dough is smooth and elastic.
Cover with a damp towel or lid to prevent drying.
checks: Dough should feel soft and spring back slowly when pressed.
If dough tears when stretched, knead 1–2 minutes more.

steps.title: Rest the dough
instructions: This relaxes gluten so rolling is easier and roti stays tender.
checks: After resting, dough should roll without snapping back too much.

steps.title: Portion and shape
instructions: Keep balls covered while you roll to prevent crusting.
checks: Smooth balls = smoother rolling and fewer cracks.

steps.title: Roll thin circles
instructions: Lightly dust a ball with flour and roll into a 6–7 inch circle (about 2 mm thick).
Use minimal dusting flour; too much can dry the roti and burn on the pan.
checks: If edges crack, dough is too dry—wet hands and knead briefly.

steps.title: Cook on hot skillet (tawa)
instructions: Heat a dry skillet over medium-high until very hot.
Place roti on the skillet; cook 20–30 seconds until small bubbles appear and the bottom shows light spots.
Flip; cook 30–45 seconds until brown spots develop.
Flip again; press gently with a spatula or cloth at the edges to encourage puffing; cook 10–20 seconds.
Remove and keep covered in a towel-lined container to stay soft.
checks: If roti browns too fast, lower heat slightly.
If roti stays pale and stiff, pan may be too cool—preheat longer.

steps.title: Optional finish and serve
instructions: Optional: brush warm rotis with ghee/butter for extra softness (not vegan).
Serve immediately with curries, dal, or chana masala.

notes: Roti is best fresh and warm.
For meal prep: freeze cooked rotis with parchment between layers.
Cool completely, then store sealed.
Reheat on skillet 15–25 seconds per side or microwave wrapped in a damp paper towel.
Freeze with parchment between rotis; store in a freezer bag.
Reheat from frozen on skillet covered briefly to steam-soften.
Keep cooked rotis covered to prevent drying and cracking.
Do not leave cooked bread at room temperature for extended periods.
After the second flip, use tongs to place roti directly over a low flame for a few seconds to puff fully.
Move constantly to avoid scorching.
Replace 1/4 cup of the water with warm milk (not vegan).
Or add 1 tsp oil/ghee to dough.
Use 1 cup atta + 1 cup all-purpose flour for softer, less wheaty roti.
You may need slightly less water.
Dough can be made earlier in the day and held covered for a few hours.
Cooked rotis reheat quickly for fast dinners.
